---
title: "Recipes and Execution Graphs"
subtitle: "Make every extraction step, input, dependency, and parameter visible before it runs."
sidebar: concepts
---

## A recipe is a saved set of extraction instructions

A call to `extract_features()` is convenient for interactive work. A recipe is
the same kind of request written as an ordinary Python dictionary that can be
inspected, tested, and saved. Its top-level `features` list contains one entry
for every extraction step.

Planning can generate the recipe for you:

```{python}
from natural_features.workflows import plan_features

plan = plan_features(
    "The scene opens.",
    features=["text.tokenize"],
)

plan.to_recipe()
```

The generated step says which extractor to `use`, gives the step a stable
`id`, connects it to `input:text`, and records its parameter values. An
extractor is the registered function or model that computes a feature. Nothing
has run yet.

## What the fields mean

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `id` | A unique name for this step, used by later steps and reports |
| `use` | The registered extractor to call |
| `inputs` | Where each extractor argument gets its value |
| `params` | Settings passed to the extractor |
| `outputs` | Optional declarations of the names and object types the step should return |
| `depends_on` | Other steps that must finish first even when their output is not passed directly |
| `postprocess` | Optional transformations applied after extraction |
| `enabled` | Whether this step participates in the run |

An input token such as `input:text` refers to an original workflow input. A
reference such as `ref:words.events` means “use the `events` output from the
earlier step named `words`.” References make data flow explicit; they are not
arbitrary strings interpreted by the extractor.

## Why the recipe becomes a graph

When one step consumes another step's output, the recipe is no longer just a
list. It is a directed acyclic graph, often shortened to DAG:

“Directed” means each connection points from producer to consumer. “Acyclic”
means the connections cannot loop back to an earlier dependency. “Graph” means
the steps may branch and rejoin instead of forming one straight chain.

The graph determines a safe execution order. A tokenizer can run before a
word-level language feature that consumes its events, while two independent
audio features can run without depending on each other.

```{python}
from natural_features.core.recipe import as_mermaid

print(as_mermaid(plan.to_recipe()))
```

The printed Mermaid text can be rendered as a flow diagram. This is useful for
reviewing a larger recipe before running expensive steps.

## Validation happens before execution

A recipe must contain a `features` list. Every enabled step needs a non-empty
registered extractor name, and every step ID must be unique. Fields such as
`params`, `inputs`, `outputs`, and `postprocess` must be mappings. References
must point to outputs that an earlier known step declares. Dependency cycles
are rejected.

After a step runs, declared output schemas are also checked. A step that
promises a `FeatureSeries` cannot silently return an `EventSeries` or an
untyped array.

## What a recipe does not provide

A recipe records what should run; it does not install an optional package or
program, download a model, or make missing credentials appear. Those runtime
choices are covered by [Execution modes](execution-modes.qmd). A recipe also
does not make a method scientifically appropriate merely because the recipe
passes structural validation.

## See also

[Preview a recipe graph](../cookbook/recipe-dag-preview.qmd) · [Catalogue and planning](catalog-and-planning.qmd) · [Execution modes](execution-modes.qmd)
